title: Write what you believe in 200 words
subtitle: A journal prompt for naming your actual values without the borrowed language
excerpt: A journal prompt for writing down what you actually believe. Not the borrowed version. Your real view. In 200 words or fewer. The word limit forces precision.

practice.whenToUse: When the question of what you actually believe — spiritually, philosophically, about how to live — feels either borrowed from elsewhere or genuinely unexplored

content.text: Most people carry beliefs absorbed from family, culture, or religion that they have never examined. Writing them down, in your own words, with a word limit that forces precision, is one of the fastest ways to see what is actually there.
What were you raised to believe about how life works? Not what you believe now. What the adults around you believed and passed on.
Which of those beliefs do you still carry? Which have you quietly let go of?

Write 200 words or fewer on what you actually believe about what happens when we die, whether there is meaning or it is made, what a good life consists of. Your version, in your language. Your version, in your language.
Read it back. Is there anything that sounds like someone else's words? Rewrite those sentences until they sound like yours.
What do you live as though you believe, even if you don't say it aloud? Does the 200 words match the behaviour?
